data.title: Realty Income: Declining Rates Make Now The Time To Buy
data.description:
Realty Income (NYSE:O), or the monthly dividend company, was recently released as our contrarian investment pick based on diverse and reliable cash flow. The company's earnings, recently released, indicate a continued ability to source valuable transactions, which as we'll see in this article, make the company a valuable investment as rates are expected to decline.

Realty Income Overview

The company is one of the largest REITs with impressive global operations.

The company has more than 50 years of operating history with a ~$81 billion enterprise value and more than $5 billion in annualized base rent. Versus its market cap, that's an almost 10% annualized based rent yield. The company's business is spread across an incredibly high credit rating due to investment grade clients and more than 15 thousand commercial real-estate properties.

The company is known as the monthly dividend company with 111 consecutive quarterly dividend increases and 661 monthly dividends at a 4.2% annualized dividend yield. It's a strong history of dividends from a diversified history.

The company has returned 13.5% compounded annually since its NYSE listing with minimal volatility. Realty Income has managed to grow its median annual AFFO by 5.5% driving this, making the company a member of the S&P 500 dividend aristocrats. More importantly, the company has a massive addressable market versus its size.

The company's estimated addressable market is ~$14 trillion, giving it continued room to grow despite its immense size.

Realty Income 2Q Results

Breaking out the specifics of its 2Q results, the company generated strong results.

The company invested $1.2 billion in the quarter at an average 7.2% cash yield taking advantage of higher interest rates that enable it to lock in higher returns. Realty Income sourced $43 billion in volume, which shows that, despite its massive scale, it could maintain a 2.7% selectivity rate with 98.6% occupancy.

O had a 103.4% rent recapture rate, showing its ability to raise rents with higher inflation rates, and it's continued to optimize its portfolio. Going into the 2H, the company expects to find additional investments with ~$5 billion in volume and ~$4.26 in AFFO.

Overall, the company's 2025 guidance is strong versus a $57.8 share price. The company expects 1% same store rent growth with flat AFFO / share and slightly lower net income. Property expenses and income taxes are expected to remain more than manageable while the company is guiding for higher investment volumes.

There is one concern here where the company's 2024 AFFO was $4.19 / share indicating only 2% YoY growth not enough to support lofty dividend increases.

Realty Income Debt

Realty Income has one of the strongest balance sheets in the industry with low-interest rate debt.

The company has 5.5x net debt to annualized pro forma adjusted EBITDA with $28 billion in net debt based on its enterprise value. The company's debt is 99.9% unsecured and 95% fixed rate, both of which make it manageable with a 6.4 years weighted average term to maturity. With expectations for the FED to decline rates, the company can refinance debt at a lower rate.

The manageable debt load is expected to fund the company's $5 billion sourcing for 2025 and enable AFFO to continue its growth.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the company's valuation. The company has a dividend yield of just under 5.6% and it uses dilutive equity to fund its business. The company expects to be able to achieve dividend growth in the mid single-digit range, but the best-case scenario here is ~10% return. With a downturn, it could be less, making the company a poor investment.

Conclusion

Realty Income is expected to see rates decline enabling the company to refinance its debt at a lower rate while continuing to earn strong returns from properties bought at a higher cap rate. The company has guided for increased acquisitions in 2025 as a result, acquisitions that it can comfortably afford to make. More so despite the size, the company has a large addressable market.

Realty Income is expecting to continue its growth and has an incredibly impressive track record of generating strong cash flow from these assets and using it to fund a multi-decade, growing dividend. Putting that all together makes Realty Income a valuable investment opportunity. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Saputo: Aged Like Fine Cheese
data.description:
Nothing like a few good months for an investment thesis to age like wine—not like milk. Well, in the case of Saputo (TSX:SAP:CA), could we say it aged like gorgonzola cheese? I think so.

I told you in April (at the height of the whole 'Trump Tariffs' mess) and reiterated in June that it was hard to ignore this cash cow.

We're talking about roughly 8% annualized returns from dividends and buybacks alone. That's right—you could've just sat tight and still beaten bonds by a wide margin, without even counting the upside.

Behind this is a global supply chain, strong brands, and margin tailwinds from years of heavy investment and cost-cutting.

That's the secret for Saputo's 66% jump in operating cash flow year over year.

Looking back at my thesis, I see that we're sitting on gains of over 27% on the stock's appreciation, far outpacing the S&P 500, which delivered a nearly 8% appreciation in the same period.

From the quarterly dividend paid in June, add at least another 0.6% and another 1.1% from buybacks in June alone.

Put it all together, and you're looking at a total return just shy of 30% in four months.

That's pretty impressive.

Of course, gems don't stay cheap forever. I had a price target of ~$30 for Saputo (9% below where the stock is trading today).

Did I let this cheese ripen too much and overdo it?

That said, I want to touch on the following points in this write-up:

  1. Take a deeper look at Q1 FY 2026 (what went on, what drove the results, and whether this momentum can pick up from here);
  2. Trying to dig a little deeper into the changing pricing of dairy products in the United States;
  3. Assess whether there's anything new in Saputo's cash generation capacity (after all, OCF jumped 66% year-over-year), and make some inferences about Capex and FCF. If there's anything special here, I'd like to update my projection regarding dividends and buybacks for FY 2026;
  4. Finally, with everything we have in hand so far, I will try to model different scenarios for Saputo and arrive at a different target price (or simply reiterate my previous one) and give my verdict for this stock.

Starting FY 2026 On The Right Foot

Saputo started FY 2026 strong—and it wasn't because of stronger sales.

In fact, Saputo reported sales of $4.6 billion, growing at least 1% year-over-year, driven by higher prices (International and Canada) and offset by both the divestiture of the Dairy Division in Australia and volatility in the milk-to-cheese spread in the United States.

Let's take a look at how this played out segment-by-segment before we move on:

  • Canada: Totaled $1.3 billion in the quarter (just over 28% of total revenue), growing 5% year-over-year. If you look closely, this represented a growth of only 10 bp sequentially. The sales increase benefited from both sales volume and pricing, across nearly all channels (retail, foodservice, and industrial). Interestingly, the pressures we saw in foodservice channels last quarter have significantly eased, with Q2 representing a relatively strong quarter for restaurants in Canada (see RBC Consumer Tracking for more information). Adjusted EBITDA for Canada was $170 million (up 11% year-over-year), maintaining a margin of 12.9%, both increasing sequentially thanks to sales leverage and cost containment;
  • United States: Sales of $2.1 billion in the quarter (just over 45% of Saputo's total for the quarter), with sales growing only 2% year-over-year. Despite a flatter market, Saputo saw benefits in both product mix and sales volume compared to the same period last year. The quarter saw new contracts signed (meal kit supplier, fast-food restaurant, and distributor), as well as a sequential stabilization in commodity prices (improving the much-talked-about negative milk-to-cheese spread) and new pricing of Federal Milk Marketing Orders (we'll look at this in more detail shortly). In any case, these factors led Saputo to report adjusted EBITDA of $171 million (up 6% year-over-year) with a margin of 8.1%, far exceeding last year's annual margin of 7%;
  • International: Sales of $865 million fell at least 14% year-over-year, now representing just over 18% of total sales. Remember, this decline was largely due to the divestiture of the Dairy Division in Australia. On the other hand, investments to reduce duplicative costs and investments in fixed assets played their part, boosting adjusted EBITDA 22% year-over-year to $55 million (6.3% margin versus 5% for the entire FY 2024), halting the incredible 41% year-over-year decline seen in adjusted EBITDA in the same period last year. They're turning things around there;
  • Europe: Revenue of $317 million, growing an incredible 20.1% year-over-year (outperforming the high-single-digit growth seen in FY 2024). Here, this result was also driven by increased sales volume (especially in private-label and bulk cheeses) as well as strategic price increases to offset inflation. The United Kingdom performed exceptionally well (Cathedral City continues to stand out). Adjusted EBITDA for the segment was $30 million (up 30% year-over-year), maintaining a 9.5% margin.

So, while not all segments showed very strong sales traction (even the largest segments like Canada and the United States didn't see explosive growth, and international sales even declined), all segments saw a strong improvement in the bottom line.

This is clearly a result of the capital investments I've been talking about for almost half a year!

To give you an idea, they reported EBITDA of $426 million, the highest in at least nine quarters (up 11.2% year-over-year).

But that wasn't all:

  1. With previous investment rounds, Saputo was able to cut duplicate SG&A costs;
  2. Interest charges decreased almost 10% year-over-year thanks to deleveraging (27% drop in debt, totaling $1.88 billion).

This caused them to report a net income of $165 million, an adjusted EPS of $0.44 (up 13% year-over-year), also driven by buybacks.

Federal Milk Marketing Order Changes: What You Need to Know

A major tailwind for Saputo in the United States is definitely the benefit of its pricing formula for raw milk producers. As you know, the company has been suffering from a negative milk-to-cheese spread (milk prices rise faster than cheese prices, compressing gross margins).

To give you an idea, in FY 2025, the average price of powdered milk soared almost 50%, far exceeding the price of butter (+3.8%) and cheese (+14.3%).

As discretionary spending remains constrained and consumers opt for private labels (generally cheaper and with tighter production margins for Saputo), profitability has suffered. Of course, this has been fully offset by recent cost-cutting initiatives, but it certainly still hinders the company's full potential.

But how would the benefit of the pricing formula for raw milk producers—implemented on June 1st of this year—help Saputo and other dairy processing industries?

In the past, the Federal Milk Marketing Orders set the minimum price milk processors had to pay farmers. That price was basically calculated by taking the value of the milk's components and multiplying it by the market price.

Within this formula, there were certain deductions called 'make allowances' (representing the costs the industry incurs to transform raw milk into products).

The big problem here is that these deductions hadn't been updated in over two decades!

With the FMMO change, 'make allowances' increased by double digits for products such as cheese, butter, skim milk powder, and whey powder.

To give you a simplified real-world example, the price paid to the producer is = value of components - make allowance. So, even if commodity prices increase, there will be a real benefit for Saputo.

For example, if milk protein sells for $4.00 a pound and the make allowance goes from $0.16 to $0.23, the price drops from $3.84 to $3.77 a pound—almost a 2% cut.

Now multiply that by the amount Saputo buys, and you're talking about millions in savings every year.

Digging into the Q1 FY 2026 earnings call, this simple change in the 'make allowance' will bring benefits of $60 to $70 million to US EBITDA for the entire FY 2026, depending on market dynamics. This would boost US EBITDA for the entire FY 2025 by double digits if the formula were implemented last year (from $615 million to somewhere between $675 and $685 million).

It's a big deal, there's no denying that.

Still Milking Cash?

Driven by EBITDA expansion and operating asset adjustments, OCF in Q1 FY 2026 was $317 million, growing over 66% year-over-year. Based on Capex of $64 million—a 34% decrease, already stabilized after years of heavier investments—FCF reached $253 million.

Let's take a look at how this has played out in recent quarters:

As you can see, in recent years:

  1. OCF has remained stable, with many quarters reporting figures above $300 million;
  2. Sequential reduction in Capex given the end of investments that boosted EBITDA in recent quarters. The negative trend in investments is clear, especially in FY 2025, reaching the lowest level in at least two years in Q1 FY 2026;
  3. With stable OCF and declining Capex, Saputo is seeing very strong FCF. It's expected to remain stable between $200 and $300 million as of Q3 FY 2025.

What's interesting is that with free cash flow climbing fast and remaining strong, Saputo is still very much a cash cow—able to reward shareholders with dividends and buybacks while also chipping away at its long-term debt.

In Q1 FY 2026 alone, Saputo returned $202 million (almost 80% of total FCF) through dividends ($79 million) and buybacks ($123 million). They also repaid $60 million in net debt.

At the end of the day, they saw a $1 million cash deficit. Regardless, they are sitting on $256 million in cash—very well positioned to continue returning that capital.

As I said earlier, I believe Saputo will be able to return at least 8% annually through dividends and buybacks.

But will that change for FY 2026? Let's take a deeper look here.

The average EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate in recent years is 78%. If we project 5-10% year-over-year EBITDA growth, we would have somewhere between $1.5 billion and $1.57 billion for FY 2026, with OCF between $1.17 billion and $1.22 billion—basically where I projected in my last write-up.

With a guided Capex of $360 million (12% lower year-over-year), we would have FCF between $1.14 billion and $1.21 billion.

Adding another color, we can project FCF of approximately $1.3 billion with the pricing improvement in the United States and some additional tailwinds seen in Q1 FY 2026.

To simplify, we would have an FCF range of $1.14 billion to $1.3 billion for FCF in FY 2026.

It's possible (but I'm not saying it's likely) that they would pull the following levers if they went all-in on each of these paths:

  1. Reduce outstanding shares by somewhere between 32 and 37 million. This would bring them to somewhere around 381.3 to 386.3 million (reducing by 7.6-8.8%, a strong shareholder yield);
  2. Continue increasing dividends above their 5-year average rate (2.4%). They currently pay $0.80 annually, spending about $330.8 million keeping them flat. Let's assume they increase them by at least 5% in FY 2026, bringing them to $0.84 per share (returning $347 million to shareholders), with a dividend yield of 2.5%. Even if they increase it by 10%, the annual dividend would be $0.88 (returning $364 million), yielding 2.6%;
  3. Repay at least $500 million in long-term debt, reducing it to $1.73 billion by the end of FY 2026.

With a midpoint FCF of $1.22 billion, I assume they'll spend $500 million repaying long-term debt. In this case, there would be $720 million left over to split between dividends and buybacks. Assuming they maintain the $320 million in dividends, there would still be about $400 million left for buybacks—similar to FY 2017.

In any case, I'm still aiming for a low-to-high single-digit shareholder yield.

As I say, Saputo is the kind of company that can pull many levers, and several at the same time.

Saputo Still 'Buy' Above $30s?

Looking at peers like Danone (OTCQX:DANOY), Emmi (OTCPK:EMLZF), Glanbia (OTCPK:GLAPF), Savencia (OTC:BNGRF), and Lifeway Foods (LWAY), we arrive at an EV/EBITDA of approximately 11.5x (approximately 11.2x for food processing companies).

As the analyst consensus estimates revenue of $19.33 billion for FY 2026 and we target an EBITDA margin between 7.5% and 8% (in line with the company's 5-year average), we would arrive at an EBITDA between $1.45 and $1.54 billion for the full year (up 1.4% to 7.7% year-over-year).

We also factor in this model:

  • Total net debt of approximately $3 billion (considering, in addition to long-term debt, capital leases and accounts payable);
  • Approximately 400 million shares outstanding at the end of FY 2026.

At this point, we could estimate a price target (depending on EBITDA for FY 2026) between $34 and $37.

But would EBITDA grow in the mid- to high-single digits? If it grew more than 11% year-over-year in Q1 FY 2026, we can model both a bull and a bear scenario to gauge sensitivity here.

In a bear scenario, we could consider flat EBITDA growing at 2% year-over-year (EBITDA between $1.43 and $1.46 billion). In this case, we could see a price target close to $33.50.

For the bull scenario, we could estimate growth of 10 to 12% year-over-year (EBITDA between $1.57 and $1.60 billion), with a price target of approximately $38.

Since I believe Saputo could continue to exceed expectations, I'm targeting something above $35—a single-digit upside. However, with another mid-to-high single-digit shareholder yield, I still see returns above 10% for this stock.

That said, Saputo's still a 'Buy' in my book.

With long-term debt coming down, I think we can count on solid returns in FY 2027 and beyond. And if the M&A market picks up, they could easily go on another buying spree.

As I always say—if a company's a strong cash generator, pulling in double-digit returns is more than enough to make the case.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Middleby Corporation: Near-Term Upside Is Limited
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on The Middleby Corporation (NASDAQ:MIDD) in Nov’24, in which I recommended a hold rating as I did not see any improvements in the CF segment fundamentals and that forward signals point to poor performance in the near term, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. Nine months on, 2Q25 results confirm that these challenges remain, despite some encouraging long-term catalysts. While the FP segment is improving and a sizable equipment replacement cycle could be a multi-year tailwind, the near-term picture is still dominated by weak QSR demand and significant tariff headwinds, which I believe will continue to cap upside potential for at least the next two quarters.

2Q25 Earnings Review

MIDD 2Q25 results were broadly in line with consensus expectations, but it still reflects a challenging operating environment. Net sales fell 1.4% y/y to $977.9 million, with mixed performance across segments. The core Commercial Foodservice [CF] segment declined 4.8% to $580.6 million, with organic sales down 5.5%. Residential Kitchen [RK] also continues to underperform, with sales down 6.1% (organic -7.8%). The only headline growth came from Food Processing [FP], up 14.4% to $216.2 million, but this was acquisition driven, as organic sales fell 2.9%. Accordingly, gross margin contracted by 30 bps y/y to 38%. The impact was more apparent at the EBITDA line, with adj. EBITDA down 7.5% to $200.2 million. This drove a 130 bps margin decline to 20.5%. As a result, adj. EPS came in at $2.35.

Green Shoots and Long-Term Catalysts

Despite the muted headline numbers, I do see several encouraging developments. Firstly, the FP segment looks to be turning a corner. Management noted that market conditions are modestly improving, with order trends steadily building throughout the year. This has driven a book-to-bill ratio above one and a larger backlog versus the start of 2025. If we combine that with a healthy M&A pipeline, I think there is a viable path for FP to be spun off as planned in 1H26 (better fundamentals make it easier to spin off). To me, this is a clear value-unlocking event, as it gives the FP business the independence it requires to continue growing the business while not being dragged down by CF and FK.

Secondly, there is a catalyst that could drive strong demand in the CF segment—a delayed equipment replacement cycle. This is probably the most important takeaway from the call: management noted that a large wave of foodservice equipment sold between 2012 and 2016 is now operating well past its typical five-to-seven-year life span. Over the years, operators have repeatedly deferred replacements due to various reasons like COVID, supply chain bottlenecks, and now tariff uncertainty. This has resulted in years of stacked pent-up demand. As maintenance costs for aging equipment rise, it is inevitable that these operators must replace their equipment, and that inflection point should come soon. My guess is that once macro conditions stabilize, this deferred cycle would translate into a multi-year tailwind for MIDD’s CF segment.

Persistent Headwinds Cloud the Near-Term Outlook

Back in my November update, the organic growth weakness in the CF segment was the key reason for my hold rating. Unfortunately, after 9 months, this weakness hasn’t improved, and it is still the main drag on performance. CF delivered another disappointing quarter of 5.5% organic sales decline, driven largely by a sharp pullback from its largest QSR chain customers. While management noted there was growth in the general market and institutional clients, I don’t think that calls for a celebration since the core customer base demand has seen severe deterioration. I expect the demand softness to persist since these QSR chains are under significant cost pressure and facing declines in same-store sales. Naturally, this led to these chains deferring equipment replacements and pushing out new store growth plans. While the timing of equipment replacement would come eventually, in the near term, I expect this demand vacuum to weigh heavily on order visibility.

On top of this demand softness is the headwind from tariffs, which has clearly weighed on earnings, as management made it clear that tariffs are the main driver of the y/y decrease in EBITDA. The estimated annualized cost impact is ~$150 million, and the worst part of this is that quarterly drag will worsen before it improves: the net negative impact on EBITDA was ~$10 million in 2Q25, and management expects this to increase to $10–$15 million in 3Q25. While MIDD has implemented pricing actions, it is not within expectations that these actions will fully offset the tariff impact until the start of FY26. In other words, the next two quarters are going to face meaningful cost pressure.

Valuation

The longer-term bull case is getting better, especially after knowing the dynamics around the upcoming replacement cycle. However, I urge readers to be cognizant that the timing of that inflection point is still murky. We don’t know if that will happen in 2026, 2027, or even 2028. But what we do know is that in the near term, say over the next 2 quarters, we are likely to see a demand vacuum for equipment replacement, and tariffs will hit earnings. In the face of these, I don’t think it makes sense to invest today. MIDD valuation is not really cheap either. As I look at it today, it trades at 15x forward PE, just slightly below its 5-year average of 16x. For now, I think the stock will be range-bound, and until there is clarity of the equipment replacement cycle starting, I doubt the stock will see any meaningful upwards traction.

Conclusion

In conclusion, while the long-term case for MIDD has seemingly gotten better, the near-term risk/reward remains unattractive. Demand from key QSR customers is likely to stay soft, and tariff-related cost pressures are expected to intensify before easing in FY26. With valuation at ~15x forward PE, only slightly below its 5-year average, I see limited scope for multiple expansion until there’s clear evidence of a demand inflection. As such, my rating for MIDD remains on hold.

data.title: Nayax: Steady Progress Toward 2028 Profit Targets
data.description:
Nayax (NASDAQ:NYAX) is up only slightly since my previous article a month ago. The company’s Q2 earnings, however, reaffirm my previous thesis about sticky customer relationships. Customer count rose 24% to ~105,000, with churn still below 3%. Net revenue retention (NRR) of 123% says that even without adding a single new customer, the existing base is spending more. This is the sort of customer relationship you want to own: they stay, they grow, and they bring you more business over time. With that in mind, I maintain my buy rating with a base case target of $48 and an upper range of $80.

Review of Q2 2025 Results

As seen above, the recurring revenue engine remains a star, and the TAM expansion ensures it is a predictable source of growth. When I looked at gross margins, my main focus was on the sequential change rather than the year-over-year (YoY) gain. The YoY comparison tells you that the company has improved against the same quarter last year, but the sequential improvement tells you whether the business is getting stronger in real time. Gross margin rose by 320 basis points from the previous quarter. That kind of movement, when repeated, signals operating leverage taking hold. A better way to frame it is to ask is whether this makes it more likely that the firm will reach its 2028 goal of a 30 percent Adjusted EBITDA margin. That would be a yes.

One of Nayax’s strengths is that it can grow both from the ground up and from the top down. In the early years, the company won customers one at a time, often small operators. Today it is also forming partnerships with equipment makers that build Nayax hardware into new machines before they ever reach the buyer.

The agreement with Autel Energy is a good example. Autel plans to embed 100,000 UNO Mini readers into AC slow chargers by the end of 2026. Each unit is a quiet entry point for recurring revenue. The hardware may not be highly profitable at the start, but once it is in place, it produces software subscription fees and payment processing income for many years.

In the payments industry, integration creates a powerful bond. Once a device is certified as part of an OEM’s machine, it is costly and disruptive to replace. That changes the contest from competing on price to building long-term relationships. This is an area where Nayax’s reach across more than one hundred countries and its experience in many industries give it a meaningful edge.

Valuation

When I value a business, I start with what management says it can earn over a reasonable period. In this case, Nayax’s target for 2028 is $1 billion in revenue with a 30 percent adjusted EBITDA margin. That works out to $300 million in EBITDA. The next step is to ask how much of that can be turned into free cash flow. Nayax expects a 50 percent conversion rate, which would mean $150 million in annual free cash flow. Given the recurring nature of its revenue, the relatively light capital needs once devices are deployed, and the low churn in its customer base, that estimate seems realistic.

The market tends to value a business on the combination of its growth and margins. Looking at comparable companies, Mastercard and Visa earn 28–31 times free cash flow with high margins and steady growth. Adyen, with strong growth and healthy margins, trades near 30 times. PayPal, with lower growth and margins, sits near 11 times. Nayax already has the highest growth rate in the group, but its margins are still developing.

For Nayax, I use a range of 12 times to 20 times free cash flow. The low end reflects a more conservative view, closer to PayPal’s multiple, and allows for slower execution or margin pressure. The high end is still below Visa or Mastercard (due to a lower EBITDA margin target) but recognizes Nayax’s ability to expand recurring revenue, improve margins, and grow its addressable market. On those assumptions, the 2028 valuation range runs from $1.8 billion to $3 billion. Compared to today’s market value, the low end offers a reasonable return while the high end offers a very good one.

Conclusion

Nayax’s second quarter confirms that the business is moving in the right direction. The valuation work above is not meant to be precise to the last dollar. It is meant to act as a gauge for deciding whether the odds are in your favor when making a reasonable long-term bet. In Nayax’s case, the range offers those odds. I am therefore maintaining a buy rating with an early target of $48.50.

data.title: Toro: Pressure From Residential Market; Initiate At Hold
data.description:
Toro (NYSE:TTC) manufactures outdoor products such as turf & landscape maintenance, golf course equipment and snow & ice management. The company is more likely to deliver another year of weak growth in FY25 due to sluggish consumer confidence and a weak housing market. I estimate its stock price is fairly valued; therefore, I am initiating with a hold rating with a fair value of $78 per share.

As shown in the charts below, Toro generates around 78% of revenue from the professional segment, which includes products used for turf maintenance, underground constructions as well as snow and ice management. Their products are used in a variety of end-markets. In addition, Toro sells its outdoor power equipment to residential market through retailers, online and direct-to-consumer channel. Due to the weak housing market and low consumer confidence, its residential business has experienced significant growth pressure, with an 11.4% revenue decline in Q2 FY25.

As depicted in the chart below, Toro has delivered strong growth during the global pandemic period, driven by robust market demands and pricing increases. In the post-Covid era, its business growth has been muted, with only 0.9% and 0.7% growth in FY23 and FY24, respectively. Due to the weak consumer confidence, the company is more likely to deliver another year of weak growth in FY25. Indeed, the company is currently in a business down cycle.

To counter the weak market demands, the company launched its AMP program, a series of cost cutting initiatives to drive margins, as shown in the slide below. As noted in its earnings call, the company has realized $70 million in cost savings so far and they were on track to deliver $100 million in total cost savings by FY27. Notably, they reduced its workforce by 10% in FY25. At the same time, they have optimized its manufacturing footprint and adjusted their product portfolio to save costs. I appreciate the management’s efforts to streamline its overhead costs and improve margins.

Toro reported its Q2 FY25 result on June 5th, posting 2% revenue decline and 1% growth in adjusted EPS, as detailed in the table below:

As discussed previously, the weak residential demands posed significant growth headwinds for the company. Its professional segment grew by 0.8% in revenue during the quarter, with weak shipments of underground and specialty construction products. The management guided for -3% to flat growth for FY25, with an adjusted EPS of $4.15 to $4.30.

Toro has repurchased $200 million of its own shares so far this year. The company has a gross debt leverage of 1.3x, slightly higher than its target range of 1-1.2x. The company has generated a total of $777 million of FCF over the past three years. Considering all these factors, I think Toro has a robust balance sheet to fund its share repurchase and dividend payout.

My key takeaway from the quarter was that the company would continue to cut costs amid weak end-market demands, while also investing in electrifications, autonomous solutions and connected technologies.

For FY25, I anticipate the company’s revenue will decline by 1.8% assuming 1% growth in the professional and 12% decline in residential segment. From FY26 onwards, I predict the company will achieve 5% normalized revenue growth, aligning with the overall market growth.

I anticipate 20bps annual margin expansion from FY26 onwards, driven by 10bps from pricing increase and 10bps from its cost-cutting initiatives. During the earnings call, the management disclosed that tariff duties would be 3% of the total annual cost of goods sold. As such, I calculate its reported operating margin will decline by 80 bps in FY25.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate the FCFF as follows:

I set the WACC to be 9.8% assuming: risk free 4.2%; beta 1.05; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $7.63 bn; debt $922 million; tax rate 19%. I assume a 4% terminal growth rate, assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume increase. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $78 per share, as per my estimate.

Key Risks

For turf maintenance equipment, Toro sustained its market-leader position in the traditional combustion engine market. However, I am not sure if Toro can secure its #1 position in the battery electric motor technology. The situation is quite similar to the automotive industry, where a traditional automaker might not survive in the age of EVs.

Toro also face cost pressure from tariffs on steel and aluminum, which are heavily used in Toro’s products. Other than raising prices, I don’t think the company can find better ways to offset these additional costs.

On August 12th, Toro announced that Edric C. Funk has been appointed President and COO, effective September 1, 2025. While Edric has been with the company for 29 years, the senior management change might cause some near-term disruptions to operations and strategic implementation.

End Note

I favor Toro’s strong market-leading positions in its product categories, and its strategic cost-cutting initiatives. The current weak consumer sentiment has posed significant growth headwinds for the company. I estimate its stock is fairly valued. As such, I am initiating with a hold rating with a fair value of $78 per share.

data.title: Romania And Bulgaria Assume Benchmark Role For Black Sea Wheat Trading
data.description:
By Paul Wightman

At a Glance

  • As the global wheat trade expands, the first futures are trading in Romanian and Bulgarian wheat.
  • Adverse weather in key grain-producing regions is contributing to supply uncertainty.

A new cash-settled futures contract connecting wheat from the export-intensive Black Sea region to the global market is off to a strong start, offering a platform to traders for risk management, arbitrage opportunities with other regional markets and potential cross-hedging strategies.

The Black Sea Wheat (CVB) Financially-Settled (Argus) futures allow for the effective pricing of wheat from one of the biggest exporting regions in the world, particularly from Romania and Bulgaria, which ships wheat from the ports of Constanța, Varna and Burgas (collectively known as CVB). Wheat from Romania and Bulgaria is shipped to destinations across the globe alongside Black Sea wheat supplies from Ukraine and Russia.

A total of around 160,000 metric tons of wheat has been traded in the first month since launch in early June 2025, and the contract is also beginning to trade on a more regular basis. Many traders expect the contract to become the leading international benchmark for the Black Sea region. Additionally, some basis trades across the Black Sea and beyond are expected to be concluded against the CVB wheat futures as the pace of trading develops (see hedging example here).

CVB Wheat, A Reliable Benchmark for Wider Black Sea Trade

The pricing and correlation of CVB wheat to other Black Sea markets make it an effective price-risk management tool for the broader region. Romania and Bulgaria have exported over 56 million tons of wheat since 2020, according to the latest data from Eurostat. Romanian and Bulgarian exports have reached around 13 million tons in the latest U.S. marketing year 2024-2025 (June-May), according to data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA). Export volumes from the CVB ports have increased 16% since 2022.

The daily Argus CVB price is well correlated to the daily Argus price of wheat exports from the Russian Black Sea port of Novorossisk. The stability in overall export levels also helps to make the CVB price a reliable price indicator for the broader Black Sea region. The latest published set of Argus daily prices through June 2025 shows a high correlation between their CVB price Free on Board (FOB) and FOB Novorossisk at 0.97, indicating that the Argus CVB wheat price (which is the basis of the CVB futures) could be readily used as a hedging instrument for cargoes loading out of Novorossisk. Typically, a value of more than 0.80 means that prices are well correlated and that traders can use the price of a commodity to hedge an underlying price exposure to the other market.

Historical Volatility in the Black Sea Wheat Markets Remains Elevated on Continued Supply Uncertainty

The 30-day historical annualized volatility, an important statistical indicator measuring the historical changes in the underlying commodity, shows that CVB wheat has traded in a range of 15% to 30% volatility since September 2023. Higher levels of price volatility typically lead to greater trading interest to manage risk against an underlying commodity.

Compounding the implications of the Russia-Ukraine war are weather-related challenges impacting the 2025 wheat harvest in parts of the Black Sea region. In Russia and Ukraine, there are expected to be smaller crops compared to previous years for the 2025-2026 marketing year. Ukraine is facing its own set of unfavorable weather conditions, with Ukrainian Agriculture Minister Vitaliy Koval estimating that 2025’s grain harvest could fall 10%. Meanwhile, Romania could see its biggest wheat crop since 1997, in part due to more favorable weather conditions. These supply uncertainties have seen heightened levels of volatility in the Black Sea wheat region.

As trading in the Black Sea continues to evolve, the role it could play for pricing on the global stage is likely to increase. Buyers of grain from the Black Sea, such as those in Asia and North Africa, could benchmark cargoes to an increasingly liquid Black Sea reference price. More broadly, as the global wheat trade continues to expand, risk management tools could play an increasingly important role for market participants across the supply chain.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Bullish And Miami International Complete The Last Major IPOs Of The Summer
data.description:
Eight issuers listed in the US this past week, six of which were eligible for inclusion in our IPO stats, joined by two SPACs. Seven IPOs and eight SPACS submitted initial filings.

Crypto exchange Bullish (BLSH) priced above the upwardly revised range to raise $1.1 billion at a $5.8 billion market cap. The company provides trading and liquidity infrastructure under its “Bullish Exchange” brand, as well as information services under its “CoinDesk” brand, which spans indices, market data, and research reports. Bullish has grown trading volume substantially, and it should benefit from a clearer regulatory environment going forward. The company soared 84% on day one and after some volatile trading, ended the week up 88%.

Financial exchange operator Miami International Holdings (MIAX) priced above the range to raise $345 million at a $2.3 billion market cap. Miami International manages options, equities, and futures exchanges, primarily under the “MIAX” banner. For the six months ended 6/30/25, average daily volume in US options on the MIAX Exchanges was 8.7 million contracts. The company has captured a growing share of the US options industry and has numerous potential new products to drive growth, but it’s heavily exposed to fluctuating trading volume. Miami International ended the week up 35%.

Indonesian travel agency NusaTrip (NUTR) priced at the low end to raise $15 million at a $75 million market cap. NusaTrip states that it is the first Indonesian-based online travel agent (OTA) in Indonesia to receive International Air Transport Association (IATA) accreditation. The company ended the week down 2%.

Israeli biotech Nasus Pharma (NSRX) priced at the low end to raise $10 million at a $74 million market cap. The company is focused primarily on the development of intranasal drugs to treat emergency medical conditions. Nasus ended the week up 1%.

Singapore-based electrical installer Magnitude International (MAGH) priced at the low end to raise $9 million at a $140 million market cap. Under the brand name BNL, the company provides electrical installation for private and public housing, commercial and mixed-use developments, and other types of public facilities. Magnitude ended the week down 63%.

Hong Kong-based event services provider BUUU Group (BUUU) priced at the low end to raise $6 million at a $66 million market cap. The company provides Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Exhibitions (MICE) solutions to a variety of clients. BUUU Group ended the week up 3%.

While not included below, Japanese osteopathic health care provider rYojbaba (RYOJ) and Australian biotech Propanc Biopharma (OTCPK:PPCB) also listed in the US this past week, but were too small to be included in our IPO stats.

Week Ahead

There are no IPOs currently scheduled for the week ahead, though some small issuers may still price.

The calendar should stay fairly quiet through the end of the month, with no major launches expected until after Labor Day. The public pipeline doesn’t provide much visibility into fall activity yet, but a few big names appear to be teeing up September listings.

After delaying in the wake of the tariff sell-off, fintech Klarna (KLAR) and ticket marketplace StubHub (STUB) are reportedly planning to go public next month. The companies both updated financials this past week.

A few major issuers also joined the pipeline this past week: crypto exchange Gemini (GEMI), transport-tech Via Transportation (VIA), and systems services provider Legence (LGN).

Street research is expected for seven companies in the week ahead, and three lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 8/14/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 14.7% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 10.8%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Reddit (RDDT) and Astera Labs (ALAB). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 31.3% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 22.1%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: ASHR: Bullish China Momentum Buttresses The Valuation Case, Eyeing $35
data.description:
It was a busy week of inflation data at home, which makes it easy to overlook what’s happening with wholesale and consumer prices abroad. Key to the US inflation story is China PPI. For many months now, the world’s second-largest economy has been exporting deflation, in a sense. It's month-over-month PPI reading continues to run in the red, which is a welcome trend for the bond market, equity investors, and the doves on the FOMC.

Today, I’m revisiting the Xtrackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:ASHR). I was bullish on the large-cap China fund last October, noting that the price-to-earnings ratio was low while stimulus efforts from Chinese fiscal authorities offered a sanguine backdrop.

ASHR has done well in the past 10 months, returning 7%, though that is about three percentage points of negative alpha to the S&P 500. I reiterate a buy rating.

China PPI Remains Sharply Negative

China's P/E Remains Depressed Both on an Absolute Basis and Relative to History

According to the issuer, ASHR seeks investment results that correspond generally to the performance, before fees and expenses, of the China Securities 300 Index (CSI 300 Index). It offers efficient access to large-cap Chinese stocks.

ASHR is a medium-sized ETF with $2.0 billion in assets under management as of August 14, 2025. That’s down materially from $3.5 billion at the time of my previous assessment. Its annual expense ratio is moderate to high at 65 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is modest at 1.0%.

But share-price momentum has improved markedly since the April low, earning the product a B ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. ASHR is quite risky, however, thanks to highly volatile price action this year and throughout most of its history. Finally, from a liquidity perspective, tradability is high; average daily volume is high at 8 million shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is just three basis points, per DWS.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the upper portion of the style box, indicating its significant exposure to Chinese large and mega-caps. From a diversification standpoint, there’s about an even split among value, blend, and growth, but there is no small- or mid-cap access.

On valuation, ASHR remains a compelling buy, in my opinion. Its price-to-earnings ratio is low at just 14.2x, while the long-term EPS growth rate is respectable at 8.3%. The resulting PEG ratio is fair at 1.7x.

ASHR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, key to ASHR’s success is strength in the bank-heavy Financials sector. Resource stocks from the Energy and Materials niches comprise just 11% of the portfolio. Information Technology, meanwhile, is a significant underweight compared to I.T.’s position in the S&P 500 (18% vs 34%).

What I like is that ASHR is not top-heavy, as the largest holding is only 3.9% of the mix.

ASHR: Holdings & Dividend Information

As for seasonality, August has historically been a dreadful month, but ASHR is bucking the long-term trend, which I take as a positive. Performance in September and October has been more mixed, but some bullish vibes have historically come about in November.

ASHR: Navigating Weak Calendar Trends Well Right Now

The Technical Take

With a low valuation, surprising persistent seasonal strength, and a diverse allocation, ASHR’s technical situation is generally healthy, reaffirming the buy call.

Notice in the chart below that shares are right at key resistance at $30. A breakout above the current level would portend a potential upside measured move price objective to slightly above long-term resistance at $35. I think that could be reached by the end of next year if the broad uptrend continues.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s below the near-term 50dma with price above both trend indicators, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph ranges in a bullish zone between 40 and 80.

Finally, with a high amount of volume by price now below the current level, there should be ample cushion if we see a downside swing into year-end.

ASHR: Shares At Resistance, Targeting Near $35 In the Quarters Ahead, Support at the 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on ASHR. The valuation case remains solid, while the technicals call for an eventual test of the 2024 peak near $35.

data.title: Cullen/Frost: Back To A Reasonable Price As Costs Continue To Weigh (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Things have been a little mixed for Cullen/Frost (CFR) in recent months. While the earnings of this quality Texan bank are tracking a little better than expected, the market's reward has been a negative 8% return for the shares since my previous update in February - a performance that is around six points worse than the wider regional bank space.

Operational trends are admittedly a little mixed under the hood. The lack of interest rate cuts this year has helped, as has robust credit quality. Revenue is also growing nicely, helped by Frost's ongoing expansion into markets like Austin. On the flip side, the costs associated with this continue to weigh on the bank's efficiency. Even so, Frost is set for over $9.40 per share in net income this year according to current sell-side consensus, which is around 6% higher than expected earlier in the year.

Turning to the shares, having downgraded them to hold on valuation concerns, I am now shifting them back to buy. Frost's valuation multiples have ticked down these past six months due to the discrepancy between its share price performance (down) and earnings and book value (both up). Meanwhile, there is some light at the end of the tunnel in terms of the pay-off from its expansion efforts, which should become more visible as we progress through the back-half of the decade.

Spreads And Credit Look Positive, Costs Less So

Looking back to my last piece, I was a little concerned that interest rate cuts would weigh on Frost this year. On the asset side, Frost's loan book skews more to floating-rate forms of commercial lending, which on the way up was good for a 60% loan beta. With interest rates now 100bps off their peak, Frost has experienced around half of that decline in terms of its down-cycle loan beta. Cash was also running at nearly 20% of the bank's assets when I last covered it.

The liability side of the balance sheet has historically been one of Frost's strongest points, with the firm enjoying one of the clearest funding cost advantages in the space. Frost was paying just 1.6% on its deposits at the peak of the cycle, mapping to a strong ~30% beta. The downside, if that's the right word to use, is that this means there is less opportunity for Frost to cut interest expenses when rates fall. Contributing to that, Frost still has an exceptional 33% of its deposits in non-interest-bearing accounts.

That is the context in which I would view Frost's down-cycle beta. While a 31% pass-through rate might not look great at first blush, it's really just a function of the bank's already-excellent deposit costs.

With the above in mind, the lack of rate cuts so far this year has probably helped. On top of that, deposit costs have fallen modestly, while there's been some movement within the asset mix that will have been a tailwind. For example, higher-yielding loans have inched up 140bps since my last piece to around 44% of overall earning assets. Weighing all that up, Frost's net interest margin is up 14 bps to a strong 3.67%.

A higher interest margin has also been complimented by the bank's ongoing organic growth. Efforts to build up its presence in markets like Dallas and Austin continue, contributing to circa 5% year-on-year growth in earning assets last quarter. Accordingly, quarterly net interest income of $429.6 million was up over 8% year-on-year.

That expansion is also powering growth in the bank's non-interest income, as more customers and deposits mean more fees from adjacent services (charges on accounts, card transaction fees, and so on). Non-interest income came in at $117.3 million last quarter, up over 5% year-on-year.

Credit quality is also a positive right now. Frost booked a 0.2% net charge-off rate over the first two quarters, which is at the low end of initial 2025 guidance. Net losses also remain around 8 bps below Frost's long-run average. Delinquencies aren't rising either. Around 0.3% of the bank's loans were 30-89 days past due at the end of last quarter - a figure that is not only lower than it was at the start of the year, but is also below its late-2010s level (before all the COVID stimulus money).

While I can't predict when there'll be a downturn, credit quality isn't really a general concern I have with this bank. Frost's net charge-off rate has been pretty strong historically, including a very good performance during the 2007-2009 crisis, when net losses peaked below 60 bps. Looking at potential trouble spots, I would note that energy lending is a much smaller part of Frost's loan book these days (around 6% versus 15% a decade ago). Besides, like I said above, loans are only 44% of Frost's earning assets, so credit quality doesn't really have the same degree of impact on its profits as it does at other banks.

Costs arguably remain Frost's weak spot right now, as expenses were up around 9% year-on-year last quarter. Again, that is related to the bank's ongoing branch expansion efforts, and it's basically a case of having to spend money to make money (it takes time for new branches to reach scale, while costs are front-loaded). On the most recent earnings call, management went into some detail on the economics, with each expansion taking around 5 years to hit profitability. Overall, Frost is about breakeven right now on this "new" part of its business.

This is making measures of its efficiency (its costs-to-assets ratio, for example) look worse than they are. Like I said last time, Frost's costs-to-assets ratio is actually higher than it was in the mid-2010s, bucking the trend in the wider space as well as other Texan banks like First Financial (FFIN). As the Dallas and Austin expansions mature over the next 1-2 years, I expect cost leverage to improve, boosting Frost's earnings growth and underlying profitability.

The Valuation

Frost trades for $124.95 as I type, down from $139.02 when I last covered it. Meanwhile, 2025 consensus EPS is up by around 6%, while its book value has risen around 8% to $63.04, aided by lower unrealized losses in the securities book. Accordingly, the stock's price/earnings ratio (based on 2025 consensus) has fallen by ~240bps to 13.25x, while the price/book ratio is down 40bps to 1.98x.

These are very reasonable multiples for this bank. Looking back, Frost has achieved high-single-digit annualized growth in its earnings per share. Book value growth looks worse on the chart, but that's a function of the unrealized losses in its securities portfolio. Strip that out, and annualized growth would also have been in the high single-digit area. Frost also pays a $1 per share quarterly dividend, equal to an ~40% payout ratio and a yield of 3.2%.

Frost's past growth fits nicely with its retained earnings ratio (60%) and return on equity (15.6% over the first half of 2025). Looking ahead, Texas is a relatively high growth state, and Frost only has a ~3.7% share of statewide deposits. I think Frost can gain market share over time. Why? Well, it consistently tops customer satisfaction surveys of the state's banks. I may be guilty of some simplification here, but that's basically what the bank's strategy is - win low-cost deposits from Texan consumers and businesses by offering superior customer service.

With that, I think Frost can carry on compounding at a high-single-digit clip over the long run. And with a 3.2% base yield from the dividend, that gets us to a circa 10%-plus annualized return. I view that as attractive for a bank with low-cost deposits and a track record of conservative underwriting. Accordingly, I am moving the shares back to buy.
